unitTitle: KS2 Y5 Introduction to vector graphics (Teach Computing — adapted)

lessons.title: The drawing tools
lessons.objectives:
I can recognise that vector drawings are made from shapes and lines
I can name the main drawing tools
I can use the shape and line tools to make a picture
I can say one way a vector drawing is different from a paper drawing
lessons.outline:
Routine first (same every lesson). Vocabulary on the board: vector · shape · line · object · toolbar · tool. TA: everyone has the starter worksheet; the drawing software (Google Drawings or PowerPoint) open on each screen. Read the four "I can…" aloud.
1. (0–10) Starter — what is a vector drawing? On the starter worksheet pupils CHOOSE that a vector drawing is made from shapes and lines, and look at a logo.
   • Support: choose what a vector is made from.  • Core: choose that it is made on a computer.  • Challenge: name a place they see vector drawings.
2. (10–20) I-do — open the shape menu, draw a rectangle; each shape is an object. Show the toolbar tools: select, line, shape, zoom. Likely error: clicking empty space deselects the shape → fix-words "click the shape once; the blue handles show it is selected."
3. (20–35) We-do — on the activity worksheet pupils LABEL the toolbar tools (label-a-diagram) and answer which shape is selected.
4. (35–50) You-do — pupils use the shape and line tools to make a simple picture (a house or robot) and save it for next lesson, then photograph it.
5. (50–55) Plenary — self-tick the four "I can…"; each pupil names one drawing tool.
Differentiation on the SAME task, low floor / high ceiling. No flashing/animation/sound on slides; movement breaks are routine.

lessons.title: Creating images
lessons.objectives:
I can identify the shapes used to make a vector drawing
I can explain that each shape in a vector drawing is an object
I can move, resize and rotate an object
I can duplicate an object to save time
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Vocabulary on the board: object · move · resize · rotate · duplicate/copy. TA: everyone has the starter worksheet; the drawing software open. Read the four "I can…" aloud.
1. (0–10) Starter — spot the shapes. On the starter worksheet pupils CHOOSE which shape was copied and that each shape is an object.
   • Support: choose the copied shape.  • Core: tick the tools that change one object.  • Challenge: say why copying is quicker.
2. (10–20) I-do — copy and paste a shape (Ctrl+C / Ctrl+V); duplicate makes more without re-drawing. Likely error: dragging a side handle stretches the shape out of shape → fix-words "use a corner handle to keep it neat."
3. (20–35) We-do — on the activity worksheet pupils ORDER the steps to copy a shape, then SORT each tool (move/resize/rotate/duplicate) to what it does.
4. (35–50) You-do — pupils build a simple picture, duplicating a shape instead of redrawing it, then photograph it.
5. (50–55) Plenary — self-tick the four "I can…"; each pupil names one tool they used.
Differentiation on the SAME task. No flashing/animation/sound on slides; movement breaks are routine.

lessons.title: Making effective drawings
lessons.objectives:
I can use the zoom tool to add detail to my drawings
I can use alignment guides to line objects up
I can use resize handles to change an object's size
I can change an object's colour or border to make a new look
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Vocabulary on the board: zoom · align · alignment guide · resize · border · modify. TA: everyone has the starter worksheet; the drawing software open. Read the four "I can…" aloud.
1. (0–10) Starter — what does zoom do? On the starter worksheet pupils CHOOSE that zoom makes the view bigger without changing the object's real size.
   • Support: choose what zoom does.  • Core: choose why zoom is helpful.  • Challenge: say why you zoom in then back to Fit.
2. (10–20) I-do — zoom in to place a small detail; show the red alignment guide and the blue size guide. Likely error: dragging a side handle squashes the shape → fix-words "corner handle keeps it the right shape."
3. (20–35) We-do — on the activity worksheet pupils ORDER the zoom steps and SORT each guide (alignment / size) to what it tells them.
4. (35–50) You-do — pupils build a picture using zoom and guides, then modify a colour or border, then photograph it.
5. (50–55) Plenary — self-tick the four "I can…"; each pupil says which guide helped.
Differentiation on the SAME task. No flashing/animation/sound on slides; movement breaks are routine.

lessons.title: Layers and objects
lessons.objectives:
I can identify that each added object creates a new layer
I can put a layer at the front or the back
I can change the order of layers in a vector drawing
I can use layering to create an image
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Vocabulary on the board: layer · object · order · front · back · arrange. TA: everyone has the starter worksheet; the drawing software open. Read the four "I can…" aloud.
1. (0–10) Starter — what is a layer? On the starter worksheet pupils CHOOSE that a new object goes on top and the first object is at the back.
   • Support: choose where a new object goes.  • Core: choose which menu moves layers (Arrange).  • Challenge: say why layer order matters.
2. (10–20) I-do — stack three shapes; the newest is on top. Show Arrange → Order → Send to back. Likely error: deleting a hidden object instead of re-ordering → fix-words "do not delete it — send the front shape to the back."
3. (20–35) We-do — on the activity worksheet pupils ORDER the Send-to-back steps and SORT front/back, using the emoji layering image.
4. (35–50) You-do — pupils build a layered picture (a face, a flower, a tree), order the layers, then photograph it.
5. (50–55) Plenary — self-tick the four "I can…"; each pupil names one shape they sent to the back.
Differentiation on the SAME task. No flashing/animation/sound on slides; movement breaks are routine. (Source layering video is 9 MB — referenced as a teacher-played hook only, not embedded.)

lessons.title: Manipulating objects
lessons.objectives:
I can select more than one object at a time
I can group objects so they move together
I can ungroup objects to change one part
I can copy and reuse a group of objects
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Vocabulary on the board: select · group · ungroup · duplicate · reuse. TA: everyone has the starter worksheet; the drawing software open. Read the four "I can…" aloud.
1. (0–10) Starter — what does group do? On the starter worksheet pupils CHOOSE that grouped objects move together and that you ungroup to change one part.
   • Support: choose how grouped objects move.  • Core: choose what to do first to change one part (ungroup).  • Challenge: say why grouping helps when copying lots.
2. (10–20) I-do — drag a box to select many objects (or Shift+click); right-click → Group; a group acts as one object. Likely error: trying to recolour one part while still grouped → fix-words "ungroup first, change it, then group again."
3. (20–35) We-do — on the activity worksheet pupils ORDER the group steps and SORT each job into group / ungroup, using the bouquet and the Ungroup menu image.
4. (35–50) You-do — pupils make one object, group it, then copy and reuse it to build a bigger picture, then photograph it.
5. (50–55) Plenary — self-tick the four "I can…"; each pupil names one thing they grouped.
Differentiation on the SAME task. No flashing/animation/sound on slides; movement breaks are routine.

lessons.title: Becoming a graphic designer
lessons.objectives:
I can use my vector skills to create a logo for a purpose
I can name the skills I used and why I used them
I can say what a graphic designer does
I can say one difference between a vector drawing and a paint drawing
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Vocabulary on the board: graphic designer · logo · purpose · vector · paint · pixel. TA: everyone has the starter worksheet; the drawing software open. Read the four "I can…" aloud.
1. (0–10) Starter — vector drawings have a job. On the starter worksheet pupils CHOOSE what a graphic designer does and TICK everywhere they see vector drawings (multi-select).
   • Support: choose who designs logos.  • Core: tick all the places (badge, label, sign).  • Challenge: say why a logo must be simple.
2. (10–20) I-do — vectors are used in documents, on signs, and printed on vans (they stay sharp at any size). Likely error: thinking vector and paint are the same → fix-words "vector = shapes you can move; paint = dots you colour in."
3. (20–35) We-do — on the activity worksheet pupils ORDER the logo steps, SORT each skill to what it does, then SORT vector vs paint.
4. (35–50) You-do — pupils design a logo for a purpose using their unit skills, group it, then photograph it.
5. (50–55) Plenary — self-tick the four "I can…"; each pupil names one vector skill they used this unit.
Differentiation on the SAME task. No flashing/animation/sound on slides; movement breaks are routine.
